---
title: "Syllabus — INFO 521: Introduction to Machine Learning"
page-layout: full
format:
  html: default
  # Nicely-styled PDF (Typst — bundled with Quarto, no TeX needed). UA navy
  # headings, red links, letter paper. Served as _site/syllabus.pdf and linked
  # from the page ("Other Formats" + the explicit link below the header).
  typst:
    papersize: us-letter
    margin:
      x: 1in
      y: 1in
    fontsize: 10.5pt
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          #set text(font: ("Helvetica Neue", "Helvetica", "Arial"))
          #show heading.where(level: 1): set text(fill: rgb("#1B4F72"))
          #show heading.where(level: 2): set text(fill: rgb("#1B4F72"))
          #show heading.where(level: 3): set text(fill: rgb("#205a80"))
          #show link: set text(fill: rgb("#AB0520"))
format-links: [typst]
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
[📄 Download this syllabus as a PDF](syllabus.pdf)
:::

**[Term / Year — no dates per design]**  ·  **Modality/meeting pattern: [confirm — in-person / online-async / hybrid]**
**Instructor: [posted per offering]**  ·  **[email]**

::: {.callout-warning title="Confirm before publishing (bracketed throughout)"}
Instructor of record + contact + office + office hours; TA (if any); term & modality;
whether FCML / PML2 / IML remain as reference texts; and all UA policy wording — this syllabus *links* to
the official UA templates rather than quoting them, so that language is never authored here.
Assessment percentages reflect the approved redesign scheme; policy prose is adapted from the
Fall 2025 INFO 521 syllabus (used with permission).
:::

---

## 1 · Course Curriculum — what will we learn?

### 1.1 Course Description
Machine learning studies algorithms that adjust their internal parameters — that "learn" — to
recognize patterns and make predictions from example data. This course introduces the
foundations of machine learning and builds them into a single probabilistic arc: from fitting a
model by minimizing **loss**, to estimating parameters by **likelihood**, to reasoning about them
with a full **posterior**, to **approximating** inference when exact answers are out of reach, and
finally to **discovering** structure without labels. Along the way students implement practical
methods for regression, classification, clustering, and dimensionality reduction in Python, and
learn to evaluate and communicate what those methods do — and where they fail — on real data.

### 1.2 Expected Learning Outcomes

**Course Learning Outcomes.** By the end of this course, students will be able to:

- **CLO1.** Identify and evaluate machine-learning approaches appropriate for regression,
  classification, clustering, and dimensionality-reduction problems.
- **CLO2.** Implement machine-learning algorithms in Python using industry-standard tools and
  workflows.
- **CLO3.** Compare and interpret model performance using appropriate evaluation metrics,
  validation techniques, and statistical reasoning.
- **CLO4.** Apply machine-learning methods to real-world datasets to develop predictive and
  descriptive models.
- **CLO5.** Communicate machine-learning results, limitations, and recommendations through
  written, visual, and technical presentations.

**Course Objectives.**

1. Introduce the core methods used in modern machine learning.
2. Give students experience implementing these methods as algorithms in Python.
3. Give students experience using and critically evaluating these methods on data.

### 1.3 Program-level relevance

**Competencies.** This course addresses the following College of Information Science competencies
for the MS in Information Science and MS in Data Science programs:

- **MS1 — Computational Thinking:** students exercise decomposition, pattern recognition,
  abstraction, and algorithmic thinking.
- **MS2 — Data Collection, Analysis, and Interpretation:** students collect, manipulate, and
  analyze data at different scales and interpret the results properly.
- **MS3 — Communication of Analytical Results:** students communicate their findings to
  interdisciplinary audiences using appropriate visualizations and technical writing.

**Other roles.** This course can serve as a core course for the MSIS Machine Learning subplan; a
general elective for the MSIS Human-Centered Computing subplan; an elective for the MSDS program
and the PhD in Information; and part of a PhD Minor in Information.

### 1.4 Course Assessment Plan
Assessment in this course is **specifications-based** ("specs grading"): work is evaluated against
clear pass/revise criteria rather than partial-credit point-picking, and most components allow
revision toward mastery. Two applied **projects** anchor the course; **homework units**, weekly
**peer-engagement loops**, periodic **checkpoints**, and a **prerequisite quiz** support and assess
the learning outcomes. See §3.1.3 for mechanics and its weights table; see the Course Schedule
(§1.6) for when each lands.

### 1.5 Course Topics
The course is organized as seven modules along one arc — *loss → likelihood → posterior →
approximation → discovery*:

1. **Foundations & Linear Regression** — the linear model, least squares, the normal equations.
2. **Learning Theory: Bias–Variance & Regularization** — model evaluation, cross-validation, the
   bias–variance tradeoff, ridge regularization.
3. **Probability, Likelihood & MLE** — probability review, the Gaussian, maximum likelihood,
   Fisher information and parameter uncertainty.
4. **Bayesian Inference** — priors, posteriors, conjugacy; the Beta–Binomial model; the conjugate
   Gaussian posterior; ridge as MAP.
5. **Approximate Inference & Bayesian Classification** — logistic regression and Newton's method;
   the Laplace approximation; a taste of MCMC / Metropolis.
6. **Support Vector Machines & Clustering** — max-margin classification and kernels (concept
   level); K-means; Gaussian mixtures and EM.
7. **Dimensionality Reduction (PCA)** — eigen-decomposition of the covariance matrix, variance
   explained, projection, and the limits of linear methods.

*(Neural networks are the natural continuation of this material and are covered in the sequel,
INFO 557; they are not part of INFO 521.)*

### 1.6 Course Schedule (week pacing — no fixed dates)
One module per week over the accelerated term. Peer loops run every week except the MLE week
(Module 3 has no activity). The two projects land on the arc's seams. Section numbers marked †
are from the PML (2022) table of contents — verify against your printing.

| Wk | Module & topic | Readings | Peer loop | Key work due |
|----|----------------|----------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | M1 · Foundations & Linear Regression | PML Ch.1 (§1.1–1.3, §1.5); MML Ch.2; MML Ch.9 (§9.1–9.2) | Least squares | Prerequisite quiz; HW Units 0–1; **Project 1 assigned** |
| 2 | M2 · Bias–Variance & Regularization | PML §4.7; PML §11.3†; MML Ch.5 | Bias–variance & ridge | HW Units 2a–2b; Checkpoint 1 (normal equations) |
| 3 | M3 · Probability, Likelihood & MLE | PML Ch.2, Ch.3, §4.1–4.2 (incl. §4.2.5), §4.7; MML Ch.6 | — | HW Unit 3; Checkpoint 2 (MLE) |
| 4 | M4 · Bayesian Inference | PML §4.6; Think Bayes Ch.2, Ch.4 (supp.: Ch.18); PML §11.7† | Bayesian updating | HW Unit 4; Checkpoint 3 (posterior); **Project 1 due** |
| 5 | M5 · Approximate Inference & Classification | PML Ch.10 (§10.1–10.2; review Ch.9); PML §4.6.8; Think Bayes Ch.19; MML Ch.7 (optional) | Logistic regression | HW Unit 5; Checkpoint 4 (Newton–Raphson); **Project 2 assigned** |
| 6 | M6 · SVM & Clustering | PML Ch.21 (§21.3–21.4); MML Ch.11; PML Ch.17 (§17.3†, optional) | K-means | Capstone HW (in progress); Checkpoint 5 (k-means / PCA) |
| 7 | M7 · Dimensionality Reduction (PCA) | MML Ch.4 (§4.2), Ch.10; PML Ch.20 (§20.1) | PCA | Capstone HW due; checkpoint retakes conclude; **Project 2 due** |

*Readings are assigned before each module (≈10–20 pages/module; see §2.5). Cross-validation
(Wk 2) and SVMs (Wk 6) are treated at concept level in lecture. Reading links live on the
[Schedule]({{< var hub_url >}}/schedule.html) page.*

---

## 2 · Course Information — what do we need to get started?

### 2.1 Instructor information
- **Instructor of record:** [posted per offering] · School of Information · [email] · [office]
- **Teaching assistant:** [if any — name / email]

### 2.2 Course communications
- Announcements are posted on **D2L**; ask questions during class/office hours (§2.3) or by email.
- Put **`[521]`** in the subject line of course email so we can find it quickly. Email the
  instructor [and TA] together for the fastest response.

**Response time.** We typically reply within one business day; we generally do not reply on
weekends or university holidays. You'll be notified in advance of any scheduling issues that may
affect response times.

### 2.3 Office Hours
Office hours are posted on the D2L course site under *Content / Course Admin*. **[Confirm times /
format.]**

### 2.4 Course Prerequisites
- Calculus I–II (derivatives and integrals; MATH 129) and Linear Algebra (MATH 313).
- Programming experience (at least three courses; the course language is **Python 3**).
- Introduction to statistics and discrete/continuous probability (ISTA 311 recommended).

If you are missing a prerequisite (or took it long ago) you may still succeed, but expect to put in
additional effort — please use office hours. An early **prerequisite quiz** serves as a
self-assessment for you and a calibration for the instructor. Multivariate calculus is helpful but
not required; relevant concepts are introduced as needed.

### 2.5 Course Format and Session Structure
- **Modality:** [confirm — in-person / online-async / hybrid].
- **Weekly rhythm:** each module delivers its lecture material (slides/notes, with interactive
  in-lecture tools), an assigned reading, a **peer-engagement loop**, and a **homework unit**.
- **Peer-engagement loops** run in four stages across the week: (1) work through the module's
  interactive tool and pass its built-in mastery check (the loop's knowledge check); (2) post a
  short reflection to your team's D2L discussion; (3) give structured peer review to **every
  member of your team**; (4) respond to the reviews on your own post and revise. **Credit
  requires completing all four stages — including replying to all of your teammates.**
- **Readings:** ≈10–20 pages before each module. Estimate ≈1 hour per 10 pages
  (<https://cte.rice.edu/resources/workload-estimator>). Tell us if that estimate is off.
- **Content:** lecture materials, external links, and textbook excerpts are posted on D2L; the
  interactive activities are hosted online and also embedded in D2L.

**2.5.1 Workload.** Plan on a **minimum of 5–6 hours per week** beyond scheduled lecture time.
Students spending substantially more should come to office hours; feedback on workload is welcome.

### 2.6 Required Resources and Equipment

**2.6.1 Required texts.** All required texts are available at no cost. We refer to them by the
acronyms below in the schedule and readings.

| Acronym | Title | Author(s) |
|---|---|---|
| **PML** | Probabilistic Machine Learning: An Introduction (2022) | Kevin Murphy |
| **MML** | Mathematics for Machine Learning | Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong |
| **Think Bayes** | Think Bayes (2nd ed.) | Allen B. Downey |
| **UDL** | Understanding Deep Learning | Simon J. D. Prince |

*Notation follows **PML** throughout the course.* **[Confirm whether to retain FCML, PML2, and IML
as optional reference texts, as in prior offerings.]**

**2.6.2 Hardware and software.** You will need a computer with:

- **Python 3** (3.11 recommended) and `pip` — <https://www.python.org>
- **Git** and a **GitHub account** (assignments are distributed via GitHub Classroom) — <https://git-scm.com>
- A **TeX distribution** (or Overleaf) for compiling PDFs — <https://www.ctan.org/starter>
- Access to **D2L** for announcements, discussions, and embedded activities.

No laptop? Borrow one from the library: <https://lib.arizona.edu/borrow/tech>.

### 2.7 Student Success Resources
- **UA resources** (accessibility & accommodations, campus safety, Campus Pantry, Campus Health,
  CAPS, Dean of Students Student Assistance, Survivor Advocacy):
  <https://catalog.arizona.edu/syllabus-policies>
- **Think Tank** — math, writing, and general tutoring: <https://thinktank.arizona.edu>
- **iSchool peer tutoring** — for course support, ask in the tutoring center's Slack `#general`
  channel whether a tutor covers this course.

---

## 3 · Course Policies — what can we agree on to learn together?

### 3.1 Progress and Completion

**3.1.1 Participation.** Participation in this course means engaging in the weekly peer-engagement
loops in good faith: completing the module activity, posting a substantive reflection on time,
giving constructive, respectful peer review to **every member of your team**, and responding to the
reviews you receive. A loop earns credit only when all stages — including replies to all teammates —
are complete. See the climate guidelines in §3.2.2.

**3.1.2 Late work, revision, and missed assessments.** This course is built around revision, so the
policies differ from a traditional exam course:

- **Homework** and **project** work is specs-graded: submissions receive **Pass** or **Not-Yet**,
  and Not-Yet work may be **revised and resubmitted** under the mechanics in §3.1.3. Automated
  "correctness" checks may be resubmitted without limit; human-reviewed components use a limited
  pool of revision tokens.
- **Peer-loop** stages are time-bound within the week because they depend on teammates; missing a
  stage cannot be made up after the loop closes, but the loop is a small (5%) component.
- **Checkpoints** are brief, scheduled derivation checks with a built-in retake (alternate
  version, the following week); see §3.1.3.
- Documented medical and family emergencies are always accommodated — contact the instructor.

**3.1.3 Grading.**

*3.1.3.1 Categories and weights.*

| Type | Category | Description | % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Summative | **Projects** (×2) | Two applied projects. *Project 1 — One Problem, Three Lenses:* one dataset through least squares, MLE, and a Bayesian posterior. *Project 2 — From Inference to Discovery:* approximate inference and classification through clustering and dimensionality reduction. | **55** |
| Formative | **Homework** | Consolidated units of math + programming exercises, specs-graded with revision. | **30** |
| Summative | **Checkpoints** (×5) | Brief, closed-book, in-class derivation checks (~15–20 min, one derivation each) that gate the project milestones. Sittings in Weeks 2–6 — one per module just taught — each retakeable (alternate version) the following week. | **9** |
| Formative | **Peer-engagement loops** | Weekly tool + reflection + peer review of every teammate + respond & revise. | **5** |
| Formative | **Prerequisite quiz** | Early self-assessment on course prerequisites. | **1** |

*3.1.3.2 Specifications grading.* Each assignment lists explicit specifications; work that meets
them earns a **Pass**, work that doesn't earns **Not-Yet** with feedback and a path to revise.
Letter grades are earned by the **bundle** of work completed at the Pass level (an A–E ladder
defined per component in the assignment's grading guide), not by accumulating partial credit.
Autograded correctness may be resubmitted freely; human-reviewed revisions draw on a limited token
pool, and a small independent-miss allowance keeps a single slip from cascading. Full mechanics are
in each assignment's grading guide and the course `GRADING` reference.

*3.1.3.3 Grading scale.*

| Grade | Range |
|---|---|
| A | 90% ≤ cumulative |
| B | 80% ≤ cumulative < 90% |
| C | 70% ≤ cumulative < 80% |
| D | 60% ≤ cumulative < 70% |
| E | cumulative < 60% |

*3.1.3.4 No "pre-grading."* The instructor will not review an entire assignment before the due date,
but is glad to answer **specific** questions. For code, isolate the problem to a line or behavior
first (use print statements!) and be ready to explain what you've already tried.

*3.1.3.5 Grade appeals.* To contest a score, email a written explanation within one week of
receiving feedback. After final letter grades post, follow the UA grade-appeal procedure:
<https://catalog.arizona.edu/policy/courses-credit/grading/grade-appeal>.

*3.1.3.6 Rounding.* Cumulative scores within 1% of a cutoff are rounded up (e.g., 89.5% → A) for
students who have completed all assignments and loops, barring documented emergencies.

*3.1.3.7 Scaling.* If assessments prove harder than intended, grades may be scaled — only ever
**upward**, never down. Scaling is not guaranteed (and unlikely unless the class average is below a
C), so proceed as if there will be none.

### 3.2 Classroom Climate and Community Engagement

**3.2.1 UA policies and student resources.** All UA courses adhere to the general UA policies:
<https://catalog.arizona.edu/policies>. Links to the Threatening Behavior policy,
Nondiscrimination and Anti-Harassment policy, Safety on Campus, and the Subject-to-Change statement
are here: <https://catalog.arizona.edu/syllabus-policies>. **[Use the current official UA
syllabus-policy language/links for the offering term.]**

**3.2.2 Course climate and inclusion.** Students and instructors share responsibility for a safe,
welcoming, inclusive environment where everyone can challenge themselves to succeed. Because much of
this course runs through team peer review, be especially considerate in written feedback: critique
the work, assume good faith, and give teammates specific, actionable, respectful comments. Disruptive
or disrespectful conduct may be referred to the Dean of Students.

**3.2.3 Academic integrity.** The UA Code of Academic Integrity
(<https://deanofstudents.arizona.edu/policies/code-academic-integrity>) and the College of Information
Science Academic Integrity Policy (<https://infosci.arizona.edu/students-and-career/academic-integrity>)
apply to this course.

*Not allowed:*

- Using course-sharing sites (Chegg, Course Hero, OneClass, StuDocu, Numerade, etc.).
- Copying text (one sentence or more) verbatim or inadequately paraphrased from any source; see
  <https://lib.arizona.edu/research/write-cite/plagiarism>.
- Copying solutions to homework or projects from online sources or from students who took the course
  previously (including solutions posted to GitHub).
- Having someone else do any part of your work.
- Including figures you did not create yourself.
- Modifying the provided automated tests to make an incorrect solution pass, or fabricating,
  sharing, or reusing another student's peer-loop completion token.

*Allowed:*

- **Generative AI tools**, provided you **disclose** how you used them (you will not lose points for
  disclosing). LLMs can hallucinate and reflect biases — you are responsible for verifying and
  critically examining anything you submit. Do not enter confidential information into prompts.
- **Collaboration** on homework, provided you name your collaborators in the submission and write up
  the work **in your own words**. Genuine peer review of teammates' loop reflections is expected and
  encouraged.
- Getting writing help from the UA Writing Center: <https://thinktank.arizona.edu/writing-center>.

*Citing sources.* Cite every resource you use — websites (with URLs), books, articles, and any human
other than the instructors. Citing "Google" is not sufficient; cite the actual resource.

*Source of truth.* This syllabus is the canonical source for the course's academic-integrity
policies; if a verbal statement conflicts with it, the syllabus governs. If a policy is unclear or
has a loophole, tell the instructor; fixes are not applied retroactively.

*Sanctions.* Plagiarism is reported to the Dean of Students with sanctions commensurate with
severity; the typical sanction is a failing grade in the course.

---

*Policy and resource language adapted, with permission, from the Fall 2025 INFO 521 syllabus.
Assessment structure, learning outcomes, competencies, topics, and schedule reflect the current
course redesign.*
